analogy: Think of "Composition and Structure of Atmosphere" through the chapter's own opening images: SOLAR RADIATION, HEAT BALANCE AND TEMPERATURE D o you feel air around you? Do you know that we live at the bottom of a huge pile of air? We inhale and exhale but we feel the air when it is in motion. You have already... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: SOLAR RADIATION, HEAT BALANCE, AND TEMPERATURE, SOLAR RADIATION, HEATING AND COOLING OF ATMOSPHERE. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Composition and Structure of Atmosphere is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that sOLAR RADIATION, HEAT BALANCE AND TEMPERATURE D o you feel air around you?

The text explains that do you know that we live at the bottom of a huge pile of air?

The text explains that we inhale and exhale but we feel the air when it is in motion.

The text explains that you have already learnt about the fact that earth is surrounded by air all around.

The text explains that this envelop of air is atmosphere which is composed of numerous gases.

The text explains that these gases support life over the earth’s surface.

The text explains that the earth receives almost all of its energy from the sun.

The text explains that the earth in turn radiates back to space the energy received from the sun.

The text explains that as a result, the earth neither warms up nor does it get cooled over a period of time.

The text explains that thus, the amount of heat received by different parts of the earth is not the same.

The text explains that this variation causes pressure differences in the atmosphere.

The text explains that this leads to transfer of heat from one region to the other by winds.

The text explains that this chapter explains the process of heating and cooling of the atmosphere and the resultant temperature distribution over the earth’s surface.

The text explains that sOLAR RADIATION The earth’s surface receives most of its energy in short wavelengths.

The text explains that the energy received by the earth is known as incoming solar radiation which in short is termed as insolation.

The text explains that as the earth is a geoid resembling a sphere, the sun’s rays fall obliquely at the top of the atmosphere and the earth intercepts a very small portion of the sun’s energy.

The text explains that on an average the earth receives 1.94 calories per sq. cm per minute at the top of its atmosphere.

The text explains that the solar output received at the top of the atmosphere varies slightly in a year due to the variations in the distance between the earth and the sun.

The text explains that during its revolution around the sun, the earth is farthest from the sun (152 million km) on 4th July.

The text explains that this position of the earth is called aphelion.

The text explains that on 3rd January, the earth is the nearest to the sun (147 million km).

The text explains that therefore, the annual insolation received by the earth on 3rd January is slightly more than the amount received on 4th July.

The text explains that hence, this variation in the solar output does not have great effect on daily weather changes on the surface of the earth.

The text explains that variability of Insolation at the Surface of the Earth The amount and the intensity of insolation vary during a day, in a season and in a year.

The text explains that the factors that cause these variations in insolation are : (i) the rotation of earth on its axis; (ii) the angle of inclination of the sun’s rays; (iii) the length of the day; (iv) the transparency of the atmosphere; (v) the configuration of land in terms of its aspect.

The text explains that the fact that the earth’s axis makes an angle of 66½ with the plane of its orbit round the sun has a greater influence on the amount of insolation received at different latitudes.

The text explains that the higher the latitude the less is the angle they make with the surface of the earth resulting in slant sun rays.

The text explains that the Passage of Solar Radiation through the Atmosphere The atmosphere is largely transparent to short wave solar radiation.

The text explains that the incoming solar radiation passes through the atmosphere before striking the earth’s surface.

The text explains that very small-suspended particles in the troposphere scatter visible spectrum both to the space and towards the earth surface.

The text explains that spatial Distribution of Insolation at the Earth’s Surface The insolation received at the surface varies from about 320 Watt/m2 in the tropics to about 70 Watt/m2 in the poles.

The text explains that the earth after being heated by insolation transmits the heat to the atmospheric layers near to the earth in long wave form.

The text explains that conduction takes place when two bodies of unequal temperature are in contact with one another, there is a flow of energy from the warmer to cooler body.

The text explains that the transfer of heat continues until both the bodies attain the same temperature or the contact is broken.

The text explains that the air in contact with the earth rises vertically on heating in the form of currents and further transmits the heat of the atmsphere.

The text explains that this process of vertical heating of the atmosphere is known as convection.

The text explains that in tropical regions particularly in northern India during summer season local winds called ‘loo’ is the outcome of advection process.

The text explains that terrestrial Radiation The insolation received by the earth is in short waves forms and heats up its surface.

The text explains that sOLAR RADIATION, HEAT BALANCE AND TEMPERATURE 69 the earth surface.

The text explains that roughly 35 units are reflected back to space even before reaching the earth’s surface.

The text explains that (iii) Compare the global distribution of temperature in January over the northern and the southern hemisphere of the earth.

The text explains that tabulate the temperature data as given in the climatological table of observatories : (i) Note the altitude, latitude of the observatory and the period for which the mean is calculated.

The text explains that (ii) Define the terms related to temperature as given in the table.

The text explains that (iii) Calculate the daily mean monthly temperature.

The text explains that sOLAR RADIATION, HEAT BALANCE AND TEMPERATURE 75 (iv) Draw a graph to show the daily mean maximum, the daily mean minimum and the mean temperature.

The text explains that (v) Calculate the annual range of temperature.

The text explains that (vi) Find out in which months the daily range of temperature is the highest and the lowest.

The text explains that (vii) List out the factors that determine the temperature of the place and explain the possible causes for temperature variation in the months of January, May, July and October.

The text explains that example Observatory : New Delhi (Safdarjung) Latitude : 28°35°’ N Based on observations : 1951 - 1980 Altitude above mean sea level : 216 m Month Mean of Mean of Highest Lowest Daily Daily Recorded Recorded Max.(°C) Min.(°C) (°C) (°C) January 21.1 7.3 29.3 0.6 May 39.6 25.9 47.2 17.5 Daily mean monthly temperature January O 21.1+7.3 = 14.2 C 2 May O 39.6+25.9 = 32.75 C 2 Annual range of temperature Mean Max.

The text explains that temperature in May - Mean Temperature in January Annual range of temperature = 32.75°C – 14.2°C = 18.55°C

Source: NCERT Class 11 Fundamentals of Physical Geography, Chapter 8: Composition and Structure of Atmosphere

prelims_traps: Do not treat SOLAR RADIATION, HEAT BALANCE and AND TEMPERATURE as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Composition and Structure of Atmosphere; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Composition and Structure of Atmosphere through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use SOLAR RADIATION, HEAT BALANCE and AND TEMPERATURE to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
